title: Never design in HTML
body:
## Interactive Design: Never Design in HTML

### This ensures that your ideas won’t be constrained by current technology; think in terms of communication, not language.

It may sound ridiculous, but it’s absolutely true. When you need to design pages, reject any limitations imposed by HTML editors, which are nothing more than automated lines of code that restrict your creative and communicative abilities. It’s best to think in terms of visual expression and use standard tools. Experiment with pen and paper; design in PageMaker, Quark, or Photoshop. Don’t limit your possibilities. The last thing you should do is limit your possibilities to HTML. While HTML has fantastic portability features as a medium for transmitting information, it is a terrible medium for design. Simply put, elements like tables, columns, and alignments are handled so poorly that they do nothing but limit the possibilities of any design, forcing us to work with bitmap images at the slightest hint of difficulty or deviation from the norm. With the arrival of layers, we’ve begun to gain some freedom, and for now, the most anticipated feature is true 256-level transparency. Sketch your designs on paper and adapt them to web-oriented design environments, such as Dreamweaver, Adobe GoLive, Fireworks, etc.—if you want to do it easily—or use Photoshop and other traditional design programs if you’re a true professional. Don’t make design decisions simply because it’s easier to do so in HTML, but because your message will be easier to receive and interpret. This way, you ensure freedom for your creativity and imagination, avoiding the inevitable similarity found in every HTML-designed page, and thus you’ll stand out in this medium. When you need inspiration, you can turn to anything except those things that limit you. As part of a team, you’ll also have access to people whose sole focus is translating images and concepts into an HTML- or CD-compatible environment. It’s important to remember that technology shouldn’t stifle our imagination, since the medium has already accustomed us to a multitude of changes and advancements, with the process of communication and expression being constantly evolving. Juan M. Estévez Art Director at Linkz Multimedia
